front.title: MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC

abstract.t: This document specifies the use of MPEG Media Transport (MMT) as a container format for Media over QUIC (MoQ). MMT provides a unified framework for streaming media over heterogeneous networks including broadcast (ATSC 3.0, ARIB STD-B60), multicast (SSM), and unicast (QUIC/WebTransport). This specification defines the mapping of MMT packets to MoQ objects, interaction with Application-Layer FEC including multi-path FEC delivery where MMTP repair packets are passed through identically across ATSC 3.0 broadcast, SSM multicast, and MoQ CDN paths, bidirectional conversion between S-TSID and MoQ catalogs, and compatibility considerations for both ATSC 3.0 and ARIB STD-B60 systems.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: MPEG Media Transport (MMT) is a transport protocol designed for delivery of multimedia content over heterogeneous networks. MMT is supported as an alternative transport in ATSC 3.0 broadcast television (Americas, South Korea) alongside the primary ROUTE/DASH transport, and is the basis for ARIB STD-B60 (Japan). MMT provides native support for:

ul.li: Media Processing Units (MPU, branded "mpuf" in ISOBMFF) as the media container, with structural similarities to fMP4
Application-Layer FEC (AL-FEC) supporting multiple schemes including RaptorQ, LDPC, and Reed-Solomon
Hybrid delivery combining broadcast and broadband

section.t: This document defines how MMT-encapsulated media can be transported over MoQ , enabling:

ol.li: Broadcast-to-Unicast bridging: Content from ATSC 3.0 or ARIB STD-B60 broadcasts can be relayed to MoQ subscribers without transcoding
Unified FEC: MMT's AL-FEC integrates with MoQ FEC repair tracks per
Multicast promotion: MoQ clients can receive SSM multicast directly when available, per
Bidirectional signaling: Convert between S-TSID (ATSC) and MoQ catalogs for seamless interoperability

section.name: Relationship to Other MoQ Media Formats
section.t: This specification complements existing MoQ packaging formats:
MMT packaging is RECOMMENDED when:

ul.li: Ingesting ATSC 3.0 or ARIB STD-B60 broadcasts
FEC protection is required for multicast delivery
Interoperability with broadcast receivers is needed

section.t: CMAF packaging (CMSF/CARP) is RECOMMENDED when:

ul.li: Interoperability with existing CDN infrastructure is needed

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: MMT Protocol -- the packet layer of MMT (ISO 23008-1 Clause 8)
Media Processing Unit -- a self-contained media segment in MMT, typically aligned with a Group of Pictures (GOP)
Media Fragment Unit -- a single coded media frame within an MPU
Service-based Transport Session Instance Description -- ATSC 3.0 signaling table containing transport and FEC parameters
Service Layer Table -- ATSC 3.0 bootstrap signaling
MMT Package Table -- signaling table containing MMT asset info
MMT Presentation Information -- presentation timing table

section.name: MMT Overview
section.t: MMT uses a layered architecture:

section.name: MMTP Header Format
section.artwork: MMTP Header (12 bytes minimum) { Version (2), Packet Counter Flag (C) (1), FEC Type (F) (2), Reserved (1), Extension Flag (X) (1), RAP Flag (R) (1), Packet Type (6), Packet ID (16), Timestamp (32), Packet Sequence Number (32), [Packet Counter (32)], // Present if C=1 [Header Extension (..)] // Present if X=1 }
section.t: Key fields for MoQ mapping:

dl.dd: Maps to MoQ track within namespace
UTC wallclock time in NTP short format, maps to MoQ object timestamp
Maps to MoQ object ID within group

section.name: Track Structure
section.t: An MMT stream maps to MoQ tracks as follows:

section.name: Object Payload
section.t: Each MoQ object carries one MMTP packet:

Publishers MAY strip the MMTP header if subscribers negotiate raw ISOBMFF delivery via catalog "container" field. When MMTP headers are stripped, the object payload contains only the MPU/ISOBMFF fragment. Note that stripping MMTP headers loses FEC Type, RAP flag, and timestamp metadata; receivers MUST rely on catalog and MoQ object headers for this information.

For MoQ unicast delivery without ATSC 3.0/ARIB interoperability requirements, LOC with MoQ extension headers is RECOMMENDED over full MMTP encapsulation. LOC eliminates ~30 bytes of per-object MMTP overhead that is redundant over MoQ (packet_id, timestamp, sequence_number, MPU fields, MFU DU header are all provided by MoQ transport framing and LOC extensions). MMTP encapsulation (container value 'mmtp') is RECOMMENDED when multicast/broadcast interoperability is required, as each UDP packet needs self-describing per-packet metadata.

section.name: Group Boundaries
section.t: Group boundaries align with MPU boundaries:

ul.li: Group N contains all MFUs of MPU N
Object 0 of each group contains the MPU metadata (mmpu/moov boxes)
The first object of each group SHOULD have RAP Flag = 1

section.name: Unified Ordering Model
section.t: The MPU Sequence Number serves as the single authoritative ordering key across all subsystems. This design ensures consistency between media decode, FEC recovery, and MoQ transport:
section.artwork: MPU Sequence Number (mpuSeq) | +-- MoQ Group ID (S4.1: mpuSeq -> Group ID) | \-- Decode order -- H.264/H.265 requires frames in sequence | +-- FEC Source Block (SBN = mpuSeq / K, per I-D.ramadan-moq-fec) | \-- ESI alignment -- source symbols map to sequential mpuSeqs | \-- MMTP Timestamp (S3.1: wallclock presentation time) \-- Presentation -- monotonic within decode order
section.t: Receivers MUST track the last decoded mpuSeq and:

ul.li: Keyframes (RAP=1): Always decode; reset decode-order tracking
Delta frames (RAP=0): Decode only if mpuSeq > lastDecodedMpuSeq; drop out-of-order delta frames to prevent reference frame mismatch
FEC repair: Use mpuSeq to derive SBN for block alignment

section.t: This ordering model applies identically across delivery paths:
The decoder MUST enforce mpuSeq ordering regardless of delivery path. While MoQ per-group delivery naturally provides ordered groups, multicast and flat-stream delivery may reorder MFUs due to:

ul.li: FEC interleaving (interleave depth > 1 causes block-level reordering)
Network path reordering (UDP provides no ordering guarantee)
CDN relay buffering (objects from different groups may interleave)

section.t: Receivers SHOULD NOT rely on the transport layer for decode ordering. The decoder is the correct enforcement point because different delivery paths (MoQ groups, multicast, broadcast) have different ordering guarantees, but the decode requirement is the same.

Implementation note: Fragment reassembly (handling FI=1,2,3 within a single MFU) and decode ordering (enforcing mpuSeq between MFUs) are separate concerns. The reassembler buffers and reorders fragments by fragment counter; the decoder tracks lastDecodedMpuSeq and drops out-of-order delta frames. This separation avoids cross-layer coupling between the container parser and the media decoder.

section.name: Media Fragment Unit (MFU) Mode
section.t: For ultra-low-latency applications, MMT supports MFU mode where each video frame is delivered as a separate unit:
section.artwork:
Standard MPU Mode: Object 0: [MMTP][MPU: mmpu+moov+moof+mdat containing all frames]
MFU Mode: Object 0: [MMTP][MPU metadata: mmpu+moov+moof header] Object 1: [MMTP][MFU: IDR frame NALUs] Object 2: [MMTP][MFU: P frame NALUs] Object 3: [MMTP][MFU: P frame NALUs] ...
section.t: MFU mode enables:

section.name: MFU Fragmentation
section.t: When MFU size exceeds typical MTU (1200-1400 bytes for QUIC), publishers SHOULD:

ol.li: Fragment MFU across multiple MMTP packets
Set Packet Counter Flag (C=1) for reassembly tracking
Publish all fragments as a single MoQ object (not multiple objects)
Include the complete MFU in the object payload

section.t: Receivers reassemble MFUs using the Packet Counter and Packet Sequence Number before media processing. The MMTP fragmentation is transparent to MoQ; each MoQ object represents a complete, potentially multi-packet MFU.
For very large frames (e.g., 8K I-frames), consider:

ul.li: Fragmenting at the MoQ object level with object dependencies

section.name: FEC Integration
section.t: MMT's AL-FEC framework supports multiple FEC schemes including RaptorQ , LDPC , and Reed-Solomon , with parameters signaled via MMTP signaling messages or, in ATSC 3.0, via S-TSID (which is part of the ROUTE transport layer). For MoQ, FEC repair uses the model defined in :
section.artwork:
Source Track: video \-- Objects: MMTP packets with FEC Type=0 or 1 (source)
Repair Track: video/repair \-- Objects: MMTP packets with FEC Type=2 (repair, passthrough)
section.t: When using MMT packaging, the repair track also uses "mmtp" packaging. Repair track objects carry raw MMTP repair packets (type 0x03) without modification per Appendix C. MoQ relays pass MMTP repair packets through as-is -- no header stripping, no re-framing, no re-encoding.

This passthrough design enables multi-path FEC delivery: the same MMTP repair packets are delivered identically via ATSC 3.0 RF broadcast, SSM multicast, and MoQ CDN. Receivers implement one MMTP parser for both source and repair tracks, extracting SBN, ESI, and OTI from the standard MMTP FEC Payload ID fields. See Appendix C for the byte-offset extraction recipe.

section.name: Interleaving
section.t: MMT AL-FEC interleaves source symbols across multiple MFUs:
Default interleave depth varies by application:

section.name: OTI Signaling
section.t: The S-TSID table contains RaptorQ OTI (Object Transmission Info):
For MoQ, OTI is signaled via FEC_CONFIG message per Section 4.

section.name: FEC_CONFIG Message
section.t: The FEC_CONFIG message and its wire format are defined normatively in Section 4.1. This document does not redefine FEC_CONFIG but specifies MMT-specific considerations for its use.

section.name: MMT-Specific FEC_CONFIG Usage
section.t: When used with MMT packaging, the FEC_CONFIG fields map as follows:

dl.dd: Typically 0x02 (RaptorQ) for ATSC 3.0 ingest, or as specified in MMTP AL-FEC signaling
Corresponds to the number of MFUs (or MMTP packets) covered by one FEC block
Number of MPU frames spanned by each FEC block, matching the original broadcast FEC interleave depth
For RaptorQ, the 12-byte concatenation of Common FEC OTI and Scheme-Specific FEC OTI per

section.t: When Repair Container is 0x01 (MMTP), per-packet OTI embedded in each MMTP repair packet (12 bytes at byte offset 21) provides immediate decoder configuration without FEC_CONFIG. FEC_CONFIG is therefore OPTIONAL for MMTP container mode; if sent, its fields are informational and SHOULD match the per-packet OTI values.
See for the complete message format, field definitions, algorithm registry, and precedence rules.

section.name: Repair Track Discovery
section.t: When FEC is enabled, the repair track uses the naming convention defined in Section 6.1:
section.artwork: Source Track: [namespace, track_name] Repair Track: [namespace, track_name, "repair"]
section.t: The subscriber MUST subscribe to the repair track separately. The repair track uses lower priority (typically 7) so repair symbols are dropped first under congestion.

section.name: Multicast Delivery of FEC_CONFIG
section.t: For multicast (SSM/ASM) delivery where bidirectional signaling is not available, FEC_CONFIG parameters are conveyed via:

ol.li: MMTP AL-FEC Signaling (message_id=0x0203): In-band delivery per ISO/IEC 23008-1:2023 Amendment 1:2025
MoQ Catalog Extension: Out-of-band delivery via catalog JSON:

section.name: Multicast Integration
section.t: MMT content can be delivered via IP multicast (SSM, AMT) and TreeDN for scalable distribution. Platform-specific delivery paths, SSM group allocation, TreeDN integration with ISP router AMT deployment, DePIN incentives, and IWA home gateway architecture are defined in .

When MMT is delivered over multicast, MMTP packets are transmitted as UDP datagrams with the standard MMTP header intact. MoQ relays at network edges terminate the multicast path and bridge MMTP content into the MoQ application layer via QUIC/WebTransport.
For ATSC 3.0 and ARIB STD-B60 receivers, MMTP over SSM is the native delivery path and requires no protocol translation.

section.name: ARIB STD-B60 Compatibility
section.t: ARIB STD-B60 (Japan's MMT-based broadcasting standard) uses the same ISO 23008-1 foundation as ATSC 3.0 with the following specific considerations:

section.name: Clock Reference
section.t: ARIB STD-B60 uses UTC wallclock timestamps in NTP short format, consistent with ISO 23008-1. The MMTP Timestamp field carries the UTC send time of the packet, which maps to MoQ object timestamps.
The MMTP Timestamp uses NTP short format (32-bit: 16-bit seconds + 16-bit fractional seconds relative to NTP epoch):
section.artwork: MoQ Timestamp (seconds) = MMTP Timestamp upper 16 bits + (lower 16 bits / 65536)
section.t: Note: This differs from MPEG-2 TS, which uses a 90kHz PTS/DTS clock.

section.name: 8K UHDTV Support
section.t: ARIB STD-B60 supports 8K UHDTV (7680x4320) via HEVC Main 10 profile at Level 6.1 (4:2:0, 10-bit). For efficient 8K delivery:

ol.li: Tiled Delivery: MoQ Group boundaries SHOULD align with HEVC CTU rows for spatial random access
Parallel Decoding: Multiple MoQ tracks MAY carry tile regions for parallel decode

section.t: Example 8K track structure:
section.artwork: namespace: "live/8k" tracks: - video/tile_0_0 (top-left quadrant) - video/tile_0_1 (top-right quadrant) - video/tile_1_0 (bottom-left quadrant) - video/tile_1_1 (bottom-right quadrant) - video/repair (FEC for all tiles)

section.name: Hybridcast Integration
section.t: ARIB defines Hybridcast for companion device synchronization (second screen experiences). When bridging Hybridcast services:

ol.li: Include timeline alignment metadata in MoQ catalog
Signal synchronization points via MoQ object timestamps

section.t: Catalog extension for Hybridcast:

section.name: Typical FEC Parameters
section.t: Publishers SHOULD preserve original FEC parameters when ingesting ARIB STD-B60 content.

section.name: Transport Hierarchy
section.t: Clients SHOULD attempt transports in preference order. The transport hierarchy for native clients (TV, mobile) and browser clients is defined in Section 6.
For MMT-specific deployments, AL-FEC () is essential on SSM/AMT paths since there is no retransmission. On MoQ/QUIC paths, FEC reduces retransmission latency but QUIC provides a reliable fallback.

section.name: Catalog Signaling
section.t: The MoQ catalog signals MMT packaging via the standard CMSF "packaging" field .

section.name: Packaging Registration
section.t: This document registers "mmtp" as a MoQ Streaming Format packaging value:
MMTP packaging indicates that each MoQ object payload contains one MMTP packet (source or repair). The MMTP header provides packet type, timestamp, sequence number, and FEC metadata natively.
Example:

When a publisher offers the same content via multiple packaging types (mmtp, loc, cmaf), each is a separate track in the catalog. The subscriber chooses which packaging to subscribe to based on its capabilities.

section.name: S-TSID to MoQ Catalog Conversion
section.t: When ingesting ATSC 3.0 content delivered via ROUTE, generate MoQ catalog from the S-TSID signaling table (defined in for the ROUTE transport layer). For MMT-delivered content, equivalent parameters are obtained from MMTP signaling messages (MPT/MPI):
section.sourcecode: S-TSID Input: <S-TSID> <RS sIpAddr="192.168.1.100" dIpAddr="232.1.1.50" dPort="5000"> <LS tsi="1" bw="5000000"> <SrcFlow rt="true"> <ContentInfo> <MediaInfo contentType="video" repId="1080p"/> </ContentInfo> <Payload codePoint="128" formatId="2"/> </SrcFlow> <RepairFlow> <FECParameters maximumDelay="1000" overhead="25" fecOTI="K=32;T=1312;Z=4"> <ProtectedObject tsi="1"> <SourceTOI x="0" y="255"/> </ProtectedObject> </FECParameters> </RepairFlow> </LS> </RS> </S-TSID>
section.t: The "multicast" field in the output uses the extended format defined in Section 7.2. Conversion rules:

section.name: MoQ Catalog to S-TSID Conversion
section.t: When generating ATSC-compatible output, convert MoQ catalog to S-TSID:
section.sourcecode: S-TSID Output: <S-TSID xmlns="tag:atsc.org,2016:XMLSchemas/ATSC3/Delivery/S-TSID/1.0/"> <RS sIpAddr="192.168.1.100" dIpAddr="232.1.1.50" dPort="5000"> <LS tsi="1" bw="5000000"> <SrcFlow rt="true" minBuffSize="5000000"> <ContentInfo> <MediaInfo contentType="video"/> </ContentInfo> <Payload codePoint="128" formatId="2" srcFecPayloadId="6"/> </SrcFlow> <RepairFlow> <FECParameters maximumDelay="133" overhead="25" fecOTI="F=32;T=1312;Z=4;N=1;Al=8"> </FECParameters> </RepairFlow> </LS> </RS> </S-TSID>
section.t: Conversion rules:

section.name: Multicast Endpoint Catalog Extension
section.t: The multicast catalog extension -- including simple and extended formats and format detection rules -- is defined in Section 7.

When converting S-TSID to MoQ catalog (), the "multicast" field in the output catalog MUST conform to the extended format defined in Section 7.2, using the "endpoints" array to represent per-TSI multicast groups.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: MMT content protection uses Common Encryption (CENC) which is preserved through MoQ transport. The MMTP header is not encrypted, allowing relays to inspect packet type and sequence without accessing media content.

section.name: Multicast Security
section.t: Multicast-specific security considerations (source authentication, replay protection, AMT relay trust) are defined in Section 8.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests registration of a MoQ Streaming Format packaging value in the registry established by :
This document also requests registration of MoQ message type (shared with ):

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
Information technology - High efficiency coding and media delivery in heterogeneous environments - Part 1: MPEG media transport (MMT)
RaptorQ Forward Error Correction Scheme for Object Delivery
Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC
Low Density Parity Check (LDPC) Staircase and Triangle Forward Error Correction (FEC) Schemes
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
MMT-Based Media Transport Scheme in Digital Broadcasting Systems
Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC

section.name: Bandwidth Comparison
section.t: FEC signaling bandwidth overhead:

section.name: Per-Object Overhead (Recurring)
section.t: Note: The MoQ Extensions row accounts for FEC PID extension (8 bytes) + Object Length extension (1-4 bytes varint). LOC source objects need zero additional extensions for FEC -- SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ group_id and object_id.

section.name: Total Overhead Analysis
section.t: For a 30fps stream with k=32 source symbols per FEC block:
FEC_CONFIG has the lowest per-session overhead and zero per-object overhead, making it ideal for unicast MoQ.
MMTP AL-FEC signaling has higher initial overhead but works without bidirectional signaling (multicast).

section.name: S-TSID Conversion Example
section.t: Complete example showing bidirectional conversion between ATSC S-TSID and MoQ catalog for a multi-track service.

section.name: Original ATSC S-TSID
section.sourcecode: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <S-TSID xmlns="tag:atsc.org,2016:XMLSchemas/ATSC3/Delivery/S-TSID/1.0/"> <RS sIpAddr="10.0.0.1" dIpAddr="232.1.1.10" dPort="5000"> <!-- Video 1080p --> <LS tsi="1" bw="8000000"> <SrcFlow rt="true" minBuffSize="8000000"> <ContentInfo> <MediaInfo contentType="video" repId="1080p" lang="en"/> </ContentInfo> <EFDT> <FDT-Instance Expires="4294967295"> <File Content-Location="video/1080p/init.mp4" TOI="1"/> </FDT-Instance> </EFDT> <Payload codePoint="128" formatId="2" srcFecPayloadId="6"/> </SrcFlow> <RepairFlow> <FECParameters maximumDelay="1000" overhead="25" fecOTI="F=32;T=1312;Z=30;N=1;Al=8"> <ProtectedObject tsi="1"> <SourceTOI x="0" y="65535"/> </ProtectedObject> </FECParameters> </RepairFlow> </LS> <!-- Audio Stereo --> <LS tsi="2" bw="128000"> <SrcFlow rt="true"> <ContentInfo> <MediaInfo contentType="audio" repId="stereo" lang="en"/> </ContentInfo> <Payload codePoint="128" formatId="2"/> </SrcFlow> </LS> </RS> </S-TSID>
